text: During the laparoscopic hernia repair, the surgeon uses the left hand to apply pressure from above, facilitating the separation of adhesions and incarcerated omentum from the hernia sac.

The surgeon uses two ports, including a harmonic scalpel and a telescope, to perform the hernia repair. Gravity aids in the separation of tissue, causing the omentum's fatty content to fall back into the abdomen as it is gently pulled with fingers.
The surgeon uses a finger to carefully pull the omentum back inside the abdomen, ensuring that the skin is not injured to prevent the risk of infection.
The surgeon continues to separate adhesions around a large hernia using laparoscopic instruments, carefully manipulating the tissue to free the incarcerated omentum.

The surgeon uses the left hand to press the hernia content completely inside the abdomen, aiding in its reduction during the eye palm hernia surgery. The sac is left intact while only the hernia content is removed.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate omental adhesions in the lower abdomen, ensuring the mesh can project six centimeters beyond the healthy margin of the hernia defect. The left hand supports the tissue from above during the procedure.

The surgeon observes adhesions on the right side where the round ligament is fixed to the anterior abdominal wall. The round ligament fixation is noted, which may be separated or left intact depending on surgical preference.

The surgeon clears adhesions from the peripheral margin around the hernia defect, preparing to apply a polyurethane mesh. The mesh features polyester on one side and polyurethane on the other, with Gore-Tex sutures at each corner. The mesh is mapped to align the defect's center with the mesh's center, and a small stab wound is made two centimeters lateral to the mesh's corner using an Alebu number knife.
The surgeon uses suture passers to perform trans-facial fixation, pulling sutures individually to secure the corners of the mesh symmetrically over the hernia defect.

The surgeon uses suture passers to individually pull and fix each corner of the mesh, ensuring that at least one centimeter of rectus muscle and peritoneum is included between the mesh and the tissue. The mesh is stretched and secured using either suture passers or a varice needle looped with vikril.
The surgeon ties the sutures subcutaneously for the third and fourth corners of the mesh. The final corner is pulled and fixed towards Palmer's point, completing the mesh fixation.

The surgeon completes the fixation of all four corners of the polyurethane mesh using transfacial sutures, ensuring bilateral symmetry and proper tension. The mesh is visibly stretched as the pressure is decreased to facilitate the final adjustments.
The surgeon uses a titanium clip and fires it around the mesh with a protack, a titanium tacker, while a grasper supports the mesh during the fixation process.

The surgeon ensures that the blue-colored side of the mesh faces towards the bowel, indicating proper orientation. The polyurethane part of the mesh is visible, and all coordinates are fixed in place.

The surgeon uses a protack device to fix the mesh to the abdominal wall, ensuring the tacker is positioned at a two-centimeter distance from the previous fixation point. The left hand supports the mesh while the tacker is fired, aligning the tip perpendicular to the abdominal wall for secure attachment, demonstrating the technique of outer crowning.
The surgeon spreads the mesh from the center to the periphery to prevent corrugation, taking care to fire the tacker either lateral or medial to the visible inferior epigastric vessel.

The surgeon performs inner crowning by decreasing the pressure on the abdominal wall to ensure proper fixation. The tacker is carefully positioned away from the defect area to avoid pricking the bare skin, which could lead to infection.
The surgical mesh is fully fixed around the hernia site, and the gas is being removed from the abdominal cavity.
